walkthrough.title: Assemble the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Viscounts set up. First, find the 5 Main Board Segments. Each segment has a number on the inside edge showing the player count side to use. Flip all 5 segments to the correct side for your player count. Now shuffle them and arrange them in a random order so the inside edges form a circle. Ignore the red numbers for now.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five main board segments arranged in a circle

walkthrough.title: Place the Castle
walkthrough.text: Grab the 3D cardboard Castle. If this is your first game, punch out all the castle pieces and assemble them as shown in the rulebook. Place the Castle in the middle of the circle of Main Board Segments so it clicks into place, locking all the segments together. The orientation of the Castle should be random. You should see three tiers: an outer first tier, a middle second tier, and an inner third tier.
walkthrough.image_caption: Assembled castle locking the board segments together

walkthrough.title: Set Up Townsfolk Card Piles
walkthrough.text: Find the 68 Neutral Townsfolk Cards. These are the cards with black titles. Give them a good shuffle and split them into 5 roughly equal piles. Place one pile faceup on each of the 5 indicated spaces around the Main Board. Remember, you are not allowed to look through these piles. Only the top card of each pile is visible.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five townsfolk piles placed around the board

walkthrough.title: Set Up Manuscript Piles
walkthrough.text: Now find all 35 Manuscripts. Separate the 5 grey-backed Manuscripts from the 30 black-backed ones. Place the 5 grey-backed Manuscripts facedown on the table, forming 5 individual piles of 1 each. Now shuffle all the black-backed Manuscripts and deal 6 facedown on top of each grey-backed Manuscript. Each pile should now have 7 Manuscripts total. Place one pile faceup on each of the 5 indicated spaces on the Main Board. The grey-backed Manuscripts should be visible on top of each pile.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five manuscript piles with grey-backed manuscripts on top

walkthrough.title: Create the Main Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all the Silver tokens, Ink Well tokens, Gold tokens, Stone tokens, and the 4 Multiplier Cards in a central supply near the Main Board where everyone can reach them. Also place the 4 Cleric Bonus Cards and the Castle Leader Card nearby. All of these resources are considered unlimited. If any run out during the game, use the Multiplier Cards to track larger amounts.
walkthrough.image_caption: Main supply with silver, resources, and bonus cards

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Components
walkthrough.text: Give each player a Player Board with the standard side faceup. Then give each player, in their chosen colour: 20 Workers, 3 Trading Posts, 3 Guildhalls, 3 Workshops, 1 Viscount meeple, 1 Corruption Marker, 1 Virtue Marker, and 8 Starting Townsfolk Cards (the ones with player-coloured titles). Place the 9 buildings on the designated spots of each Player Board. Place the Corruption Marker on the far right and the Virtue Marker on the far left of the Player Board track.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player board with buildings, markers, and workers

walkthrough.title: Choose the Start Player
walkthrough.text: Randomly decide on a start player and give them the Start Player Marker. This marker will stay with that player for the entire game. It does not rotate. Turn order goes clockwise from the start player.
walkthrough.image_caption: Start player marker

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Player Cards and Hero Townsfolk
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the 10 Player Cards and lay out a number of cards in a line equal to the number of players plus 1. So for a 3-player game, lay out 4 Player Cards. Then shuffle the 10 Hero Townsfolk Cards (the ones with brown titles) and place 1 Hero Townsfolk Card below each Player Card, forming pairs.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player cards paired with hero townsfolk cards

walkthrough.title: Draft Player Cards in Reverse Order
walkthrough.text: In reverse turn order, starting with the player to the right of the start player and moving counter-clockwise, each player selects one pair of cards and does the following: Place their Viscount on the numbered space of the Main Board matching the number on their chosen Player Card. Gain all resources, Silver, Debts, and Deeds listed on the Player Card. Resolve any additional effects at the bottom of the Player Card. Flip the Player Card over and place it next to your Player Board as a reference. Shuffle the Hero Townsfolk Card together with your 8 Starting Townsfolk Cards and any Neutral Townsfolk Cards gained during setup to form your facedown Draw Pile. Draw 3 cards from your Draw Pile as your starting hand.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player selecting a card pair and placing their viscount

walkthrough.title: Set Up Poverty and Prosperity Piles
walkthrough.text: Find the Poverty Card and Prosperity Card. On top of the Poverty Card, place a number of Debt Cards (unpaid-side faceup) based on the player count shown on the Poverty Card's reference. Place all remaining Debt Cards below the Poverty Card. Do the same for the Prosperity Card with Deed Cards (acquired-side faceup) on top and remaining Deed Cards below. Note that Debt and Deed counts may not be equal at all player counts. You are now ready to play.
walkthrough.image_caption: Poverty and prosperity piles with debt and deed cards

summary.title: Assemble the Main Board
bullets: Flip 5 Main Board Segments to correct player-count side
Shuffle and arrange in random order forming a circle
Ignore red numbers on inside edges

summary.title: Place the Castle
bullets: Assemble 3D cardboard Castle (first game: punch out pieces)
Place in centre of board segments — clicks into place
Random orientation — has 3 tiers (first, second, third)

summary.title: Set Up Townsfolk Card Piles
bullets: Shuffle 68 Neutral Townsfolk Cards (black titles)
Split into 5 roughly equal faceup piles on indicated board spaces
Do not look through piles — only top card visible

summary.title: Set Up Manuscript Piles
bullets: Separate 5 grey-backed Manuscripts from 30 black-backed ones
Place 1 grey-backed Manuscript facedown, then stack 6 shuffled black-backed on top of each (7 total per pile)
Place 5 piles faceup on indicated board spaces — grey-backed visible on top

summary.title: Create the Main Supply
bullets: Place Silver, Ink Wells, Gold, Stone, and Multiplier Cards centrally
Place 4 Cleric Bonus Cards and Castle Leader Card nearby
All resources considered unlimited — use Multiplier Cards if supply runs out

summary.title: Distribute Player Components
bullets: Each player: 1 Player Board (standard side up), 20 Workers, 1 Viscount
Each player: 3 Trading Posts, 3 Guildhalls, 3 Workshops (placed on Player Board)
Each player: 1 Corruption Marker (far right), 1 Virtue Marker (far left)
Each player: 8 Starting Townsfolk Cards (player-coloured titles)

summary.title: Choose the Start Player
bullets: Randomly select start player — give them the Start Player Marker
Marker stays with this player for the entire game (does not rotate)
Play proceeds clockwise

summary.title: Lay Out Player Cards and Hero Townsfolk
bullets: Shuffle 10 Player Cards, lay out (player count + 1) in a line
Shuffle 10 Hero Townsfolk Cards (brown titles), place 1 below each Player Card
Forms pairs — 1 Player Card + 1 Hero Townsfolk Card each

summary.title: Draft Player Cards in Reverse Order
bullets: Reverse turn order: start right of Start Player, go counter-clockwise
Select 1 pair: place Viscount on matching board space, gain listed resources/Debts/Deeds
Resolve any additional Player Card effects, then flip card as reference
Shuffle Hero + 8 Starting Townsfolk + any gained Neutrals into Draw Pile, draw 3 cards

summary.title: Set Up Poverty and Prosperity Piles
bullets: Poverty Card: stack Debt Cards (unpaid-side up) on top per player count reference, remaining below
Prosperity Card: stack Deed Cards (acquired-side up) on top per player count reference, remaining below
Counts may differ between Debts and Deeds at different player counts

walkthrough.title: Game Overview and Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: Viscounts of the West Kingdom is played over an undetermined number of rounds. Each round, every player takes one turn, starting with the player holding the Start Player Marker and proceeding clockwise. On each turn you move through 6 phases, laid out from left to right on your Player Board: Phase 1 is Card Management, Phase 2 is Movement, Phase 3 is Primary Action, Phase 4 is Recruit, Phase 5 is Resolve Collision, and Phase 6 is Draw Cards. The game ends when either the Poverty Card or the Prosperity Card is revealed from their respective piles.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player board showing the six phases left to right

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Card Management
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, you manage the line of Townsfolk Cards on your Player Board. First, move all cards currently on your board one space to the right. If a card drops off the right side, place it faceup into your discard pile and resolve any drop-off effect it has. Then place one Townsfolk Card from your hand into the leftmost space of your Player Board. If it is a Criminal, you gain 1 Corruption for each Criminal icon on your Player Board. Resolve any immediate effects on the played card. The card you play also determines your movement in Phase 2, based on the Silver value printed at its top-left corner.
walkthrough.image_caption: Cards shifting right on the player board

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Movement
walkthrough.text: Move your Viscount clockwise around the Main Board a number of spaces equal to the Silver value of the card you just played. You must move at least that many spaces, but you can pay 1 Silver per additional space to move further. Follow the arrows leaving your current space. The board has an outside path and an inside path, and your Viscount can switch between them at junctions. You cannot move backwards. If your Viscount lands on the same space as an opposing Viscount, that opponent may immediately rearrange the Townsfolk Cards on their own Player Board.
walkthrough.image_caption: Viscount moving clockwise with path options

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: The Four Primary Actions
walkthrough.text: Now you take one of four primary actions based on where your Viscount stands. On the outside path you may Trade or Construct a Building. On the inside path you may Place Workers or Transcribe a Manuscript. Each action is powered by specific icons on the Townsfolk Cards on your Player Board. You can also dismiss the faceup Townsfolk Card adjacent to your Viscount on the Main Board by paying its Silver cost — this removes the card from the game but gives you its icons for this action and its immediate effect. You may only dismiss one card per turn. Criminals are wild icons that count as any icon type for primary actions.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four primary action icons

walkthrough.title: Primary Action: Trade
walkthrough.text: Trade can only be done on the outside path. Count your Merchant icons and Criminal icons on your Player Board. You can spend Silver (1 per extra Merchant icon) or dismiss a Townsfolk for more. The exchange depends on your Viscount's location. Possible exchanges include: 1 Merchant icon gains 1 Silver. 2 Merchant icons gain 1 resource (Ink Well, Gold, or Stone). 3 Merchant icons let you destroy 1 Townsfolk Card from your hand or draw pile, gaining Silver equal to its value. 4 Merchant icons let you flip 1 Debt or 1 Deed. You can repeat the exchange as many times as you can afford.
walkthrough.image_caption: Trade exchange rates by merchant icon count

walkthrough.title: Primary Action: Construct a Building
walkthrough.text: Constructing can only be done on the outside path. Count your Builder icons and Criminal icons. You can spend Stone (1 per extra Builder icon) or dismiss a Townsfolk for more. There must be an empty Building Spot at your Viscount's location. A river divides each segment's building spots: left spaces only access left-side spots, and right spaces only access right-side spots. Building costs are: Workshop requires 3 Builder icons, Trading Post requires 5, and Guildhall requires 7. Place the building from your Player Board onto the chosen spot. You immediately gain the effect shown on that Building Spot. If the building links to an adjacent constructed building, both players at each end of the link gain the printed link bonus.
walkthrough.image_caption: Building placement with river dividing spots

walkthrough.title: Building Abilities and Scoring
walkthrough.text: Each building you remove from your Player Board reveals an ongoing ability for the rest of the game. Trading Posts give: free extra movement, reduced dismiss cost to 1 Silver, and a permanent icon. Guildhalls each give a permanent icon on your Player Board. Workshops give: a discard ability when hiring, 1 Virtue during collisions if no Criminals on your board, and increased hand limit by 1. Building VP scoring is cumulative: Workshops score 3 or 5 VP for 1 or 2 built. Trading Posts score 4, 9, or 15 VP for 1, 2, or 3 built. Guildhalls score similarly. The more of a type you build, the more points you gain.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player board showing building abilities revealed

walkthrough.title: Primary Action: Place Workers in the Castle
walkthrough.text: Placing Workers can only be done on the inside path. Count your Noble icons and Criminal icons. You can spend Gold (1 per extra Noble icon) or dismiss a Townsfolk for more. Use 1, 3, 5, or 8 Noble icons to place 1, 2, 3, or 4 Workers into the first tier of the Castle section adjacent to your Viscount. You must have Workers in your supply. After placing, if you have 3 or more of your Workers in any first-tier section, move 1 Worker inward to the second tier (gaining its effect), then move 1 Worker clockwise and 1 counter-clockwise around the first tier. Repeat for any new groups of 3. Then check the second tier: 3 or more Workers move 1 inward to the third tier, gaining any 1 resource.
walkthrough.image_caption: Castle tiers showing worker movement inward

walkthrough.title: Castle Details: Bumping, Leader, and Scoring
walkthrough.text: After all Castle movements, check if any first or second tier section has more than 3 Workers of any colour. The current player chooses which Workers to bump until only 3 remain. Workers bumped from the first tier: owner gains 2 Silver. Workers bumped from the second tier: owner gains 1 Virtue and any 1 resource. Workers can never be bumped from the third tier. The first player to move a Worker into the third tier claims the Castle Leader Card, worth 5 VP at game end and granting +1 hand limit. If another player later has more Workers in the third tier, they take the card. At game end, each Worker in the Castle scores VP equal to its tier (1, 2, or 3).
walkthrough.image_caption: Castle bumping rules and leader card

walkthrough.title: Primary Action: Transcribe a Manuscript
walkthrough.text: Transcribing can only be done on the inside path. Count your Cleric icons and Criminal icons. You can spend Ink Wells (1 per extra Cleric icon) or dismiss a Townsfolk for more. The Manuscript adjacent to your Viscount shows how many Cleric icons are required. Pay the cost and take the Manuscript, placing it alongside your Player Board. Resolve any immediate effects. You may only transcribe 1 Manuscript per turn. The first player to transcribe 3 Manuscripts of the same ribbon colour claims the corresponding Cleric Bonus Card worth 3 VP. Manuscripts score VP from sets of different ribbon colours: 1 colour scores 1 VP, 2 different colours score 4 VP, and 3 different colours score 9 VP. You can have multiple sets.
walkthrough.image_caption: Manuscript with cleric cost and ribbon colours

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Recruit
walkthrough.text: After your primary action, you may hire the faceup Townsfolk Card adjacent to your Viscount on the Main Board. Pay its Silver cost (shown at top-left), place it into your discard pile, and resolve its immediate effect (top-right of card). You can only hire 1 Townsfolk during this phase. If the Townsfolk pile at your location is empty, there are pre-printed Townsfolk on the board that cannot be hired but can still be dismissed for their icons and effects.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hiring a townsfolk card

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Resolve Collision
walkthrough.text: If your Virtue and Corruption Markers are now on the same space of your Player Board, you must resolve a collision. Follow this order: First, all players who have built their second Workshop and have no Criminals on their Player Board gain 1 Virtue. Second, resolve any collision effects from Townsfolk Cards on Player Boards. Third, you gain the items printed directly above where your markers sit. Fourth, all opponents gain items printed directly below your markers, if applicable. After resolving everything, reset both Corruption and Virtue Markers to the far ends of your Player Board.
walkthrough.image_caption: Virtue and corruption markers colliding

walkthrough.title: Virtue, Corruption, and Criminals
walkthrough.text: Throughout the game you will gain Virtue and Corruption from various actions and effects. When you gain Corruption, move your Corruption Marker 1 space to the right. When you gain Virtue, move your Virtue Marker 1 space to the left. If the markers cannot move further, extra movements are ignored. If you gain both at the same time, resolve Corruption first. Once the markers collide, they stick together and move as one unit. Criminals are wild icons for primary actions but cause Corruption: you gain 1 Corruption for each Criminal icon on your Player Board when you play a Criminal card. When you shuffle your discard pile into a new Draw Pile, if you have any Criminal icons on your board you gain 1 Corruption; if none, you gain 1 Virtue.
walkthrough.image_caption: Virtue and corruption track on player board

walkthrough.title: Debts and Deeds
walkthrough.text: Throughout the game you gain Debts and Deeds from collisions and other effects. Debts come from the pile on the Poverty Card and Deeds from the Prosperity Card. The game end triggers when either card is revealed. At game end: each unpaid Debt costs you 2 VP. Each acquired (unflipped) Deed scores 1 VP. Flipping a Debt from unpaid to paid immediately gives you any 1 resource and avoids the -2 VP penalty. Flipping a Deed from acquired to approved upgrades it to 3 VP instead of 1. If the Poverty Card is revealed, the players with the most flipped Deeds score 12, 8, and 4 VP for first, second, and third. If the Prosperity Card is revealed, the same applies for flipped Debts.
walkthrough.image_caption: Debt and deed cards with flip states

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: Draw Cards and End of Game
walkthrough.text: Draw cards from your Draw Pile until you reach your hand limit (starts at 3, can be increased). If your Draw Pile is empty, shuffle your discards to form a new pile and check for Criminals as described. You may already be at or above your hand limit, in which case do not draw. After drawing, the next player begins their turn. The game ends once the Poverty or Prosperity Card is revealed. Finish the current round, then play one final round so all players have equal turns. The player to the right of the Start Player Marker always has the last turn. It is possible for both cards to be revealed, creating a Divided Kingdom where both Poverty and Prosperity bonuses apply.
walkthrough.image_caption: Draw pile and discard pile

walkthrough.title: End-Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up your score across these categories: Constructed Buildings as printed on your Player Board. Workers in the Castle, VP per Worker equal to their tier. Transcribed Manuscripts from sets and printed VP. Castle Leader Card (5 VP) and Cleric Bonus Cards (3 VP each). Unpaid Debts lose 2 VP each. Acquired Deeds gain 1 VP each. Approved Deeds gain 3 VP each. Poverty Card bonus for most flipped Deeds (12/8/4 VP). Prosperity Card bonus for most flipped Debts (12/8/4 VP). In games with fewer than 3 players, the middle 8 VP reward is skipped. Highest total wins. Tiebreaker is most remaining Silver and resources.
walkthrough.image_caption: Scoring summary reference

summary.title: Game Overview and Turn Structure
bullets: Undetermined number of rounds — each player takes 1 turn per round, clockwise
6 phases per turn: Card Management, Movement, Primary Action, Recruit, Resolve Collision, Draw Cards
Game ends when Poverty Card or Prosperity Card is revealed from its pile

summary.title: Phase 1: Card Management
bullets: Shift all Player Board cards 1 space right — dropped card goes to discard (resolve drop-off effect)
Play 1 Townsfolk Card from hand to leftmost space (resolve immediate effect)
Playing a Criminal: gain 1 Corruption per Criminal icon on your Player Board
Card's Silver value = your movement in Phase 2

summary.title: Phase 2: Movement
bullets: Move Viscount clockwise by the card's Silver value (minimum)
Pay 1 Silver per additional space beyond minimum
Outside path and inside path — follow arrows, no backtracking
Landing on opponent's Viscount lets them rearrange their cards

summary.title: Phase 3: The Four Primary Actions
bullets: Outside path: Trade or Construct a Building
Inside path: Place Workers or Transcribe a Manuscript
Icons on Player Board cards + dismissed Townsfolk fund actions
Criminal icons are wild — count as any icon for primary actions
May dismiss 1 adjacent Townsfolk (pay Silver cost) for extra icons + immediate effect

summary.title: Primary Action: Trade
bullets: Outside path only — powered by Merchant + Criminal icons
1 icon = 1 Silver, 2 icons = 1 resource, 3 icons = destroy 1 card, 4 icons = flip 1 Debt or Deed
Spend Silver for extra Merchant icons (1:1) or dismiss Townsfolk
Repeat exchange as many times as affordable

summary.title: Primary Action: Construct a Building
bullets: Outside path only — powered by Builder + Criminal icons
Workshop = 3 icons, Trading Post = 5 icons, Guildhall = 7 icons
Must have empty Building Spot at Viscount's location (River divides left/right access)
Gain Building Spot effect + Link bonus if connecting to another player's building
Spend Stone for extra Builder icons (1:1)

summary.title: Building Abilities and Scoring
bullets: Trading Posts: +1 free movement, dismiss cost = 1 Silver, permanent icon
Guildhalls: each provides a permanent icon on Player Board
Workshops: discard on hire, +1 Virtue during collision (if no Criminals), +1 hand limit
Cumulative VP: more of same type = more points (e.g. 3 Trading Posts = 15 VP)

summary.title: Primary Action: Place Workers in the Castle
bullets: Inside path only — powered by Noble + Criminal icons
1/3/5/8 Noble icons = place 1/2/3/4 Workers in first tier adjacent to Viscount
3+ Workers in first tier: move 1 inward to second tier (gain effect), move 1 CW and 1 CCW
3+ Workers in second tier: move 1 inward to third tier (gain any 1 resource)
Spend Gold for extra Noble icons (1:1)

summary.title: Castle Details: Bumping, Leader, and Scoring
bullets: After movements: bump Workers over 3 per section (current player chooses who)
Bumped from first tier = 2 Silver, bumped from second tier = 1 Virtue + 1 resource
Castle Leader Card: first to third tier claims it (5 VP + 1 hand limit) — changes if surpassed
End-game: each Worker scores VP equal to tier (1/2/3)

summary.title: Primary Action: Transcribe a Manuscript
bullets: Inside path only — powered by Cleric + Criminal icons
Pay Cleric icons shown on adjacent Manuscript, spend Ink Wells for extras (1:1)
1 Manuscript per turn — resolve immediate effects
First to get 3 of same ribbon colour claims Cleric Bonus Card (3 VP)
Manuscript sets: 1 colour = 1 VP, 2 colours = 4 VP, 3 colours = 9 VP (multiple sets allowed)

summary.title: Phase 4: Recruit
bullets: May hire 1 faceup Townsfolk adjacent to your Viscount (pay Silver cost)
Place hired card in discard pile, resolve immediate effect
Empty piles show pre-printed board Townsfolk — cannot hire, but can dismiss

summary.title: Phase 5: Resolve Collision
bullets: Only triggers if Virtue and Corruption Markers are on the same space
Order: (1) Workshop 2 owners with no Criminals gain 1 Virtue, (2) collision card effects, (3) you gain items above markers, (4) opponents gain items below
After resolving: reset both markers to opposite ends of Player Board

summary.title: Virtue, Corruption, and Criminals
bullets: Corruption moves right, Virtue moves left — resolve Corruption first if simultaneous
Once markers collide, they stick and move together until collision resolved
Criminals: wild for primary actions, but cause Corruption when played or when shuffling deck with Criminals on board
Shuffling with no Criminals on board = 1 Virtue instead

summary.title: Debts and Deeds
bullets: Debts come from Poverty pile, Deeds from Prosperity pile — revealing either triggers game end
Unpaid Debt = -2 VP, Acquired Deed = 1 VP, Approved Deed = 3 VP
Flipping a Debt to paid: gain 1 resource immediately
Poverty Card revealed: most flipped Deeds scores 12/8/4 VP (1st/2nd/3rd)
Prosperity Card revealed: most flipped Debts scores 12/8/4 VP

summary.title: Phase 6: Draw Cards and End of Game
bullets: Draw to hand limit (starts at 3, increases with buildings/Castle Leader)
Empty Draw Pile: shuffle discards into new pile, gain 1 Corruption if Criminals on board or 1 Virtue if none
Game end: finish current round + 1 final round (equal turns for all players)
Both cards revealed = Divided Kingdom (both Poverty and Prosperity bonuses apply)

summary.title: End-Game Scoring
bullets: Buildings (as printed on Player Board), Workers (VP per tier), Manuscripts (sets + printed VP)
Castle Leader Card (5 VP), Cleric Bonus Cards (3 VP each)
Debts: -2 VP unpaid, Deeds: 1 VP acquired / 3 VP approved
Poverty Card: 12/8/4 VP for most flipped Deeds; Prosperity Card: same for flipped Debts
Tiebreaker: most remaining Silver and resources

walkthrough.title: Welcome and Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with 3 players — Red, Blue, and Green. Red holds the Start Player Marker. Each player has drafted their Player Card, placed their Viscount on the Main Board, gained starting resources, and drawn 3 cards into their hand. We will see each player take 2 turns so you can watch the full turn cycle twice and spot some common mistakes. Ready? Let's go.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three players set up and ready to begin

walkthrough.title: Red's First Turn: Card Management
walkthrough.text: Red is up first. In Phase 1, Red has no cards on the Player Board yet since it is the first turn. Red looks at their hand of 3 cards and picks a Merchant with a Silver value of 2. They place it in the leftmost space of their Player Board. This Merchant has an immediate effect that grants 1 Silver from the supply. Red collects it. Now on to movement.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red places first card on player board

walkthrough.title: Red's First Turn: Movement and Trade
walkthrough.text: The Merchant has a Silver value of 2, so Red must move their Viscount exactly 2 spaces clockwise, or pay Silver to move further. Red moves 2 spaces and lands on an outside path space. Red decides to take the Trade action. They have 1 Merchant icon on their Player Board. The exchange at this space is 1 Silver per Merchant icon. Red gains 1 Silver. Not a huge haul, but a solid opening move. Red does not hire the adjacent Townsfolk this turn — saving Silver for later. No collision to resolve. Red draws back up to 3 cards. Turn done.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red moves viscount and trades

walkthrough.title: Blue's First Turn: A Common Mistake
walkthrough.text: Blue is excited about getting Workers into the Castle early. They play a Noble card with a Silver value of 3. They move their Viscount 3 spaces and land on the inside path. Great, they can place Workers. But here is the problem. Blue only has 1 Noble icon on their Player Board. That is enough to place just 1 Worker. Blue spends no Gold because they forgot to check their resources during planning. One Worker goes into the first tier. It does not trigger any Castle movement because you need 3 Workers in a section for that. Blue essentially spent their whole turn placing a single Worker that scores just 1 VP.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blue places a single worker — inefficient

walkthrough.title: Why Blue's Move Was Wasteful
walkthrough.text: Placing a single Worker is one of the weakest things you can do in Viscounts. The Noble action scales dramatically: 1 icon for 1 Worker, but 3 icons for 2 Workers and 5 icons for 3. Those extra Workers trigger Castle chain reactions that cascade inward, giving you resources and potentially bumping opponents. Blue should have either played a different card to target a Trade or Build action, or spent Gold to boost their Noble count to at least 3. Always plan your card play around how many icons you can muster for your intended action.
walkthrough.image_caption: Noble action scaling reminder

walkthrough.title: Green's First Turn: Smart Opening
walkthrough.text: Green plays a Builder card with Silver value 2 and moves to an outside path space with an empty Building Spot. Green has 2 Builder icons on their Player Board from the card plus their Hero Townsfolk ability. They spend 1 Stone to get a third Builder icon, reaching the 3 required for a Workshop. Green places their Workshop onto the Building Spot and gains its immediate effect: 1 Ink Well. The Workshop also reveals an ongoing ability on their Player Board: a Discard ability whenever they hire a Townsfolk. Nice opening — Green got a building down early.
walkthrough.image_caption: Green constructs a workshop

walkthrough.title: Green Recruits a Townsfolk
walkthrough.text: In Phase 4, Green looks at the faceup Townsfolk adjacent to their Viscount. It is a Cleric card that costs 2 Silver to hire and has an immediate effect of gaining 1 Virtue. Green pays 2 Silver, places the Cleric into their discard pile, and gains 1 Virtue. Because Green built their first Workshop this turn, they also get to use the Workshop's Discard ability: they optionally discard 1 card from their hand to thin their deck. Green discards a weak Starting Townsfolk. A powerful chain of effects from one turn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Green hires a cleric and uses workshop ability

walkthrough.title: Round 1, Turn 2: Red Builds Up
walkthrough.text: Back to Red for their second turn. Red shifts their Merchant card one space to the right and plays a Builder from hand into the leftmost space. The Builder has Silver value 1, so Red moves 1 space clockwise to an outside space with an empty Building Spot. Red has 1 Builder icon from the card and decides to dismiss the adjacent Townsfolk. It is a Woodcutter costing 2 Silver to dismiss. Red pays 2 Silver, gains the Woodcutter's 1 Builder icon plus its immediate effect of 1 Virtue. That gives Red 2 Builder icons. Red spends 1 Stone for a third, constructs a Workshop, and gains the Building Spot effect. Solid.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red dismisses townsfolk to fund construction

walkthrough.title: Blue's Second Turn: Another Mistake
walkthrough.text: Blue wants to make up for the weak first turn. They play a Criminal card from their hand. Immediately, Blue must gain 1 Corruption for each Criminal icon on their Player Board. That is 1 Corruption, moving their Corruption Marker one space right. Blue moves their Viscount to the inside path and wants to transcribe a Manuscript. The Manuscript requires 4 Cleric icons, but Blue only has the 1 Criminal icon (which counts as wild). They have no Ink Wells to spend and no Cleric-bearing Townsfolk to dismiss. Blue cannot take this action at all. They are forced to take a weaker action instead — they place their 1 Criminal as 1 Noble icon and put a single Worker in the Castle. Another weak turn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blue stuck without enough cleric icons

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Icons Before Playing Cards
walkthrough.text: Blue's mistake was playing a card without checking whether they could actually fund the action they wanted. Before you place a card in Phase 1, count how many matching icons you will have after placing it. Factor in resources you can spend (Stone for Builder, Gold for Noble, Ink Wells for Cleric), and check whether the adjacent Townsfolk can be dismissed for extra icons. If the numbers do not add up, play a different card. Criminals are tempting because they are wild, but they come with Corruption and only provide 1 icon. Do not rely on a single Criminal to power a big action.
walkthrough.image_caption: Planning icons before playing

walkthrough.title: Green's Second Turn: Transcribing a Manuscript
walkthrough.text: Green plays a Cleric card and moves to the inside path. Green now has 2 Cleric icons on their Player Board — the newly played Cleric plus the Hero card's icon. The adjacent Manuscript costs 3 Cleric icons. Green spends 1 Ink Well (gained earlier from the Building Spot) for the third icon. They take the Manuscript and resolve its immediate effect: gain 1 Deed. Green is now making progress on multiple tracks — buildings, manuscripts, and deeds. After the primary action, Green hires a Townsfolk in Phase 4, gaining another useful card for their deck.
walkthrough.image_caption: Green transcribes a manuscript

walkthrough.title: Blue's Recovery Path
walkthrough.text: After two weak turns, Blue needs a plan. The key insight is that Blue tried to do too many different things — Workers on turn 1, Manuscripts on turn 2 — without building up icons for either. Blue should pick one primary strategy and stack icons for it. If going for the Castle, play Noble cards and stockpile Gold. If going for Manuscripts, play Cleric cards and collect Ink Wells. Every card you play should contribute to your next big action. Blue decides to focus on building next, saving up Builder icons and Stone. Better late than never.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blue refocusing strategy

walkthrough.title: How Collisions Would Work
walkthrough.text: No one triggered a collision this round, but let's talk about what would happen. Suppose Red's Corruption Marker had moved right from Criminals and their Virtue Marker had moved left from building effects, until both markers sat on the same space. In Phase 5, Red would resolve the collision. First, check if any player has Workshop 2 and no Criminals — they would gain 1 Virtue. Then resolve any collision card effects. Then Red gains the items printed above the marker position — this might be a Deed, Silver, or resources depending on the space. All opponents gain items printed below. Finally, both markers reset to opposite ends. Collisions are powerful — they are your main source of Debts and Deeds.
walkthrough.image_caption: Collision resolution example

walkthrough.title: Round Summary
walkthrough.text: After 2 turns each, here is where everyone stands. Red has 1 Workshop built and some Silver and resources. Green is in the best position with 1 Workshop, 1 Manuscript, and a growing deck of useful Townsfolk. Blue had 2 weak turns but learned important lessons about icon planning and action focus. The game continues with each player taking more turns, building toward scoring in buildings, manuscripts, Castle workers, and deed/debt management. The end game will trigger when those Poverty or Prosperity piles run out.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round 1 recap

walkthrough.title: Key Lessons from Round 1
walkthrough.text: Three takeaways from this round. First: always count your icons before choosing which card to play. The card you place determines both your movement and your available icons. Second: placing 1 Worker is almost always wasteful. Aim for at least 3 Noble icons to place 2 Workers and trigger Castle chain reactions. Third: pick a lane early. Spreading across Trade, Build, Castle, and Manuscripts without enough icons for any of them leads to weak turns. Focus your deck and resources on one or two primary strategies.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three key lessons

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all four primary actions in practice, watched Blue learn from two common mistakes, and seen how Green's focused strategy led to a strong opening. You understand how card management drives movement and actions, how icons fund everything you do, and how Criminals offer power at the cost of Corruption. You are ready to continue playing on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for beginner strategy and common traps to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a glossary of every townsfolk type, building, resource, and game term.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Welcome and Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 players: Red (start), Blue, Green
Covers 2 full turns per player to demonstrate the turn cycle
Blue makes common mistakes to highlight what to avoid

summary.title: Red's First Turn: Card Management
bullets: First turn: no cards to shift — place 1 card from hand to leftmost space
Card's Silver value determines movement in Phase 2
Resolve immediate effects when playing a card

summary.title: Red's First Turn: Movement and Trade
bullets: Move Viscount clockwise by card's Silver value (can pay extra)
Trade action on outside path: 1 Merchant icon = 1 Silver
Choosing not to recruit saves Silver for future turns

summary.title: Blue's First Turn: A Common Mistake
bullets: Blue places Workers with only 1 Noble icon — just 1 Worker placed
No Castle chain reaction (needs 3+ Workers in a section)
Whole turn spent for 1 VP of Castle presence

summary.title: Why Blue's Move Was Wasteful
bullets: Noble action scales: 1 icon = 1 Worker, 3 icons = 2, 5 = 3, 8 = 4
Extra Workers trigger cascade movements inward + resources
Always aim for 3+ Noble icons or choose a different action

summary.title: Green's First Turn: Smart Opening
bullets: Green builds a Workshop using 2 Builder icons + 1 Stone
Gains Building Spot effect (1 Ink Well) + reveals ongoing ability
Early building = early ability unlock

summary.title: Green Recruits a Townsfolk
bullets: Hires Cleric for 2 Silver — gains 1 Virtue from immediate effect
Workshop's discard ability lets Green thin their deck
Chaining building ability with recruit for maximum value

summary.title: Round 1, Turn 2: Red Builds Up
bullets: Red dismisses adjacent Townsfolk for extra Builder icon + immediate effect
Pays 2 Silver to dismiss, gains 1 Virtue + 1 Builder icon
Constructs Workshop with 2 icons from cards + 1 Stone

summary.title: Blue's Second Turn: Another Mistake
bullets: Blue plays Criminal: gains 1 Corruption for each Criminal on board
Wants to transcribe but only has 1 icon (Criminal) vs 4 required
Forced into weak fallback action — another near-wasted turn

summary.title: Lesson: Plan Your Icons Before Playing Cards
bullets: Count icons BEFORE placing card in Phase 1
Factor in resources (Stone, Gold, Ink Wells) and dismiss options
Criminals are wild but provide only 1 icon with Corruption cost

summary.title: Green's Second Turn: Transcribing a Manuscript
bullets: Green uses 2 Cleric icons + 1 Ink Well to meet Manuscript cost of 3
Takes Manuscript, resolves immediate effect (1 Deed)
Progress on buildings, manuscripts, AND deeds

summary.title: Blue's Recovery Path
bullets: Blue tried Workers then Manuscripts — no focus, no icon buildup
Fix: pick one strategy and stack icons + resources for it
Every card you play should contribute to your next big action

summary.title: How Collisions Would Work
bullets: Collision = Virtue and Corruption Markers on same space in Phase 5
Resolve: Workshop 2 check, card effects, you gain top items, opponents gain bottom
Reset both markers to ends — collisions are your main Debt/Deed source

summary.title: Round Summary
bullets: Green leads: 1 Workshop, 1 Manuscript, growing deck
Red solid: 1 Workshop, resources stockpiled
Blue behind but learning — icon planning is key

summary.title: Key Lessons from Round 1
bullets: Count icons before choosing which card to play
Placing 1 Worker is almost always wasteful — aim for 3+ Noble icons
Pick a lane: focus your deck and resources on 1-2 strategies

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have practiced all four primary actions
Watched Blue learn from two common mistakes (weak Worker placement, no icon planning)
Understand how card management drives movement, icons, and strategy

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips for beginner strategy and common traps
Reference the Appendix for every townsfolk type, building, and game term
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Card Play Every Turn
walkthrough.text: The single most important decision each turn is which card you play to your Player Board in Phase 1. This one choice determines three things: your movement distance (the card's Silver value), the icons available for your primary action, and whether you gain Corruption from playing a Criminal. Before placing a card, look at where your Viscount will land, what action you want to take, and how many matching icons you will have. If the math does not work, play a different card.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Spread Too Thin
walkthrough.text: Viscounts has four primary actions, and new players often try all of them in the first few turns. This is a trap. Each action requires a critical mass of matching icons to be effective. Building a Workshop needs 3 Builder icons, placing 2 Workers needs 3 Noble icons, and transcribing a Manuscript can need 4 to 6 Cleric icons. Pick one or two action types as your focus and build your deck around those icon types. Your Townsfolk recruitment, resource stockpiling, and card management should all feed into your chosen strategy.

walkthrough.title: Understand Criminal Risk and Reward
walkthrough.text: Criminal cards are wild icons that count as any type for primary actions. This makes them powerful and flexible. However, every Criminal you play to your Player Board gives you 1 Corruption per Criminal icon already showing. And every time you shuffle your discard pile with Criminals on your board, you gain 1 Corruption. This speeds up collisions, which can be good or bad. Collisions give you Debts and Deeds, which are essential for scoring. But too much Corruption without a plan means unpaid Debts piling up at -2 VP each. Use Criminals deliberately, not recklessly.

walkthrough.title: Recruit and Dismiss Townsfolk Wisely
walkthrough.text: Hiring Townsfolk in Phase 4 adds them to your deck. This grows your deck, which sounds good but can dilute your icon focus. Only hire cards that match your strategy — if you are building, hire cards with Builder icons. Dismissing Townsfolk costs Silver but gives you their icons for one action plus their immediate effect. This is often more efficient than hiring. A key tip: dismissing removes cards from the game, which thins the shared supply and denies cards to opponents. Think of dismissing as both action fuel and denial.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Debts and Deeds Early
walkthrough.text: Do not ignore Debts and Deeds until end-game scoring. Unpaid Debts cost 2 VP each, which adds up fast. Flipping a Debt to paid costs you nothing extra and gives you any 1 resource as a bonus. Meanwhile, flipping Deeds from acquired to approved triples their value from 1 VP to 3 VP. Look for Trade actions at the 4-icon level to flip Debts and Deeds. Also watch the Poverty and Prosperity piles — when either is getting low, the players who have been flipping cards will score big bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Use the Castle Strategically
walkthrough.text: The Castle is where Workers score VP equal to their tier (1, 2, or 3 VP each). Placing Workers is expensive in Noble icons, so make it count. Aim for 3+ Noble icons to place at least 2 Workers, triggering the cascade that moves a Worker to the second tier and rewards you with resources. Two Workers placed does nothing special — you need 3 in a section to trigger movement. The Castle Leader Card (5 VP + hand limit) is valuable but not worth chasing at the expense of other strategies. If you commit to the Castle, commit fully with Noble-heavy cards and Gold reserves.

walkthrough.title: Build Early for Ongoing Abilities
walkthrough.text: Buildings score points based on how many of each type you construct, but they also unlock powerful ongoing abilities for the rest of the game. The first Trading Post gives free extra movement. The first Workshop gives a discard ability when hiring. The first Guildhall gives a permanent icon on your Player Board. Getting even one building down in the first few turns gives you compounding value over the rest of the game. Prioritize building early, even if it means spending resources aggressively.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Board Paths Carefully
walkthrough.text: Your Viscount's position determines which actions are available. Outside path gives you Trade and Build. Inside path gives you Place Workers and Transcribe. When you play a card, its Silver value is your minimum movement. If the math puts your Viscount on the wrong path for your desired action, you will need to spend Silver to move further or plan differently. Before committing to a card, trace the path: can your Viscount reach a space where the right action is available and where there is a useful Townsfolk or Manuscript adjacent? Path planning separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Deck Thinning Is Powerful
walkthrough.text: Your starting deck has 9 cards, many of which are mediocre. Destroying or discarding weak cards makes your deck cycle faster and more consistently. The Trade action at the 3-Merchant-icon level lets you destroy cards and gain Silver equal to their value. Workshop 1 lets you discard when hiring. These abilities let you remove weak starting cards so your strong Townsfolk and Hero card come up more often. A lean 7-card deck where every card supports your strategy is far better than a bloated 15-card deck full of off-strategy filler.

walkthrough.title: Trigger Collisions at the Right Time
walkthrough.text: Collisions happen when your Virtue and Corruption Markers meet. The position where they collide determines what you gain — different spaces on the track give different combinations of Debts, Deeds, Silver, and resources. You have some control over collision timing through how many Criminals you play and how much Virtue you gain. Ideally, you want collisions to happen when the items above the collision space align with your needs. Early collisions give weaker rewards but more frequent cycling. Late collisions give stronger rewards but fewer of them. Finding the right rhythm for your strategy is key.

summary.title: Plan Your Card Play Every Turn
bullets: Card choice determines movement, available icons, and Corruption risk
Check Viscount destination, desired action, and icon count BEFORE placing
If the math doesn't work, play a different card

summary.title: Do Not Spread Too Thin
bullets: Pick 1-2 primary actions and build deck/resources around those icons
Workshop = 3 Builder, 2 Workers = 3 Noble, Manuscripts = 4-6 Cleric
Recruitment, resources, and card management should all feed your strategy

summary.title: Understand Criminal Risk and Reward
bullets: Criminals are wild icons for primary actions — very flexible
Cost: 1 Corruption per Criminal on board when playing one, plus Corruption on shuffle
Speed up collisions (Debts/Deeds) but risk unpaid Debts at -2 VP each
Use deliberately, not recklessly

summary.title: Recruit and Dismiss Townsfolk Wisely
bullets: Hire only cards matching your icon strategy — avoid deck dilution
Dismissing costs Silver but gives icons for one action + immediate effect
Dismissing removes cards from the game = opponent denial
Hiring adds to discard, not hand — takes time to draw

summary.title: Manage Your Debts and Deeds Early
bullets: Unpaid Debts = -2 VP each, Approved Deeds = 3 VP (vs 1 VP unflipped)
Flipping a Debt also gives you any 1 resource — free bonus
Trade at 4 Merchant icons to flip Debts/Deeds
Watch Poverty/Prosperity piles — flipping scores bonuses when cards revealed

summary.title: Use the Castle Strategically
bullets: Workers score VP = their tier (1/2/3) at game end
Need 3+ Workers in a section to trigger cascade movement
Castle Leader Card = 5 VP + hand limit, but changes if surpassed
If committing to Castle, go all-in with Noble icons and Gold

summary.title: Build Early for Ongoing Abilities
bullets: Buildings unlock permanent abilities: free movement, cheaper dismiss, extra icons
Even 1 early building compounds value over the whole game
Building VP is cumulative — more of same type = exponentially more points

summary.title: Watch the Board Paths Carefully
bullets: Outside path: Trade and Build; Inside path: Workers and Transcribe
Card Silver value = minimum movement — trace the path before committing
Check: right action available? Useful Townsfolk/Manuscript adjacent?
Spend Silver for extra movement when needed to reach optimal spaces

summary.title: Deck Thinning Is Powerful
bullets: Starting deck has mediocre cards — destroy or discard the weak ones
Trade at 3 Merchant icons destroys cards (gain Silver = card value)
Workshop 1 lets you discard when hiring — passive deck thinning
Lean 7-card deck > bloated 15-card deck with off-strategy filler

summary.title: Trigger Collisions at the Right Time
bullets: Collision position determines Debts, Deeds, Silver, and resources gained
Control timing via Criminal play (speeds Corruption) and Virtue effects
Early collisions = weaker rewards, more frequent; Late = stronger, fewer
Align collision rewards with your strategy needs

walkthrough.title: Castle Domination Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Castle-heavy strategy is one of the highest-ceiling approaches in Viscounts. The goal is to get as many Workers as possible into the second and third tiers, where they score 2 and 3 VP each. To execute this, stack Noble icons on your Player Board through Townsfolk with Noble icons and Guildhalls that provide permanent Noble icons. Stockpile Gold to spend as extra Noble icons. Aim for 5 or 8 Noble icons per placement to put 3 or 4 Workers in at once, triggering chain reactions that cascade Workers inward. The Castle Leader Card (5 VP) is a natural bonus. The key is that each Worker placement triggers movements that push Workers toward the third tier, creating a snowball effect.

walkthrough.title: Manuscript Collection Engine
walkthrough.text: The Manuscript strategy focuses on Cleric icons and Ink Wells to transcribe high-value Manuscripts. Manuscripts score in two ways: the VP printed on the tile itself, and set bonuses from collecting different ribbon colours (1/4/9 VP for 1/2/3 colours per set). Three full sets of 3 colours each would score 27 VP from sets alone. The first player to get 3 Manuscripts of the same ribbon colour also claims a Cleric Bonus Card worth 3 VP. Recruit Townsfolk with Cleric icons, build the Guildhall that provides a permanent Cleric icon, and stockpile Ink Wells through Trade actions. Timing matters — Manuscript piles deplete and do not refill.

walkthrough.title: Building-Focused Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Building strategy aims to construct all 9 buildings for maximum VP from the Player Board scoring track. Trading Posts score 4/9/15 VP for 1/2/3 built. Three of each type is 15+15+5 = 35 VP from buildings alone (plus the Workshop 5 VP). This requires Builder icons and Stone. The key advantage of building is the ongoing abilities you unlock. Three Trading Posts give you free movement, cheap dismissals, and a permanent icon. Three Guildhalls give three permanent icons. Three Workshops give deck thinning, collision Virtue, and hand limit increase. Build order matters — usually Workshop first for the discard ability, then Trading Posts for movement and economy.

walkthrough.title: Criminal-Heavy Corruption Engine
walkthrough.text: An advanced strategy involves deliberately running many Criminals to trigger frequent collisions. Criminals are wild icons, making your actions more flexible. The Corruption they generate pushes you toward collisions faster. Collisions give you Debts and Deeds from the track, plus Silver and resources. The key insight: the middle and late positions on the collision track give the best rewards, including multiple Deeds and Debts. By controlling how many Criminals you play and when you gain Virtue, you can aim for collisions at the most rewarding positions. The risk is accumulating unpaid Debts. Pair this with Trade actions to flip Debts, converting a -2 VP penalty into a neutral or positive outcome with a free resource bonus.

walkthrough.title: Deck Building and Thinning
walkthrough.text: Your deck is your engine. Every card in it either helps or hurts your strategy. Advanced players aggressively thin their decks to 6-8 cards, ensuring every draw is useful. Destroy weak starting cards through the Trade action at 3 Merchant icons. Use Workshop 1 to discard when hiring. Only recruit Townsfolk that match your icon needs — a Cleric player should not hire Merchant cards just because they are available. The tighter your deck, the faster you cycle through it, and the more consistently you hit your desired action each turn. Watch for Townsfolk with powerful ongoing or drop-off effects and build your deck around them.

walkthrough.title: Exploiting Building Links
walkthrough.text: When you construct a building that links to an opponent's building, both players gain the link bonus. This means building next to opponents can be mutually beneficial. But it also means you should consider defensive placement. If you are behind in buildings, construct in spots that do not link to the leader. If you are ahead, build where links benefit you more than opponents. Some link effects are very strong: 2 Silver, a resource, or a Deed/Debt flip. Experienced players plan their building route around the main board to maximize link bonuses from their own buildings while minimizing gifts to opponents.

walkthrough.title: Poverty vs Prosperity Race
walkthrough.text: The endgame trigger and its scoring bonus create a strategic tug-of-war. When the Poverty Card is revealed, the players with the most flipped Deeds score 12/8/4 VP. When the Prosperity Card is revealed, the same applies for flipped Debts. This means you should track what other players are flipping. If you have many flipped Deeds, you want the Poverty pile to run out — so focus actions that generate Debts (taking them depletes the Poverty pile). Conversely, if you have flipped Debts, push toward Prosperity by gaining Deeds. Both can be revealed in the same game (Divided Kingdom), which rewards players who have been flipping both types.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Endgame
walkthrough.text: Because the game ends when Poverty or Prosperity is revealed, you have some control over game length. If you are in a strong position, accelerate the endgame by taking actions that deplete the trigger pile. Gain extra Debts or Deeds through collisions. If you are behind, slow it down by avoiding actions that pull from the dwindling pile. Count the remaining cards in both piles and plan accordingly. Remember: after the trigger, the current round finishes plus one final round. This means the start player has a structural advantage, since they guaranteed the most total turns.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Collision Rewards
walkthrough.text: The collision track has different reward tiers. The outer positions (where markers start closer to the edges) give weaker rewards like 1 Silver and 1 Debt. The inner positions give much stronger rewards like 2 Deeds, 3 Silver, and a resource. You can manipulate where collisions happen by controlling the rate of Corruption and Virtue gains. To aim for inner positions, gain Corruption and Virtue in measured amounts so the markers meet near the centre. Cards with ongoing collision effects (triggered during Phase 5) add extra value — recruit these specifically if you plan to collide frequently. The second Workshop's collision Virtue bonus rewards having no Criminals on your board during collisions.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: At a competitive level, Viscounts rewards reading what opponents are doing. Track which icons opponents are accumulating, what buildings they have constructed, and where their Viscounts are headed. If an opponent is building heavy, consider whether building next to them helps or hurts you. If someone is racing through the Poverty pile, evaluate whether you should compete for flipped Deeds or pivot to another scoring path. Watch Townsfolk piles too — if a pile near your opponents has strong cards for their strategy, consider dismissing those cards to deny them. Board awareness separates reactive players from proactive ones.

summary.title: Castle Domination Strategy
bullets: Stack Noble icons + Gold for 5-8 icon placements (3-4 Workers at once)
Chain reactions cascade Workers inward: first tier to second to third
Workers score VP = tier (1/2/3), Castle Leader Card = 5 VP
Guildhalls provide permanent Noble icons to fuel the engine

summary.title: Manuscript Collection Engine
bullets: Cleric icons + Ink Wells fund transcription (costs range from 3-6)
Set scoring: 1 colour = 1 VP, 2 = 4 VP, 3 = 9 VP per set
First to 3 same-colour Manuscripts claims Cleric Bonus Card (3 VP)
Manuscript piles deplete and do not refill — timing matters

summary.title: Building-Focused Strategy
bullets: All 9 buildings built = 35+ VP from Player Board scoring alone
Ongoing abilities compound: free movement, cheap dismiss, permanent icons, hand limit
Build order: Workshop first (discard ability), then Trading Posts (economy)
Requires Builder icons and Stone stockpile

summary.title: Criminal-Heavy Corruption Engine
bullets: Criminals = wild icons + faster collisions from Corruption
Middle/late collision positions give the best rewards (multiple Deeds/Debts)
Pair with Trade at 4 icons to flip Debts: -2 VP penalty becomes neutral + free resource
Risk: unpaid Debts pile up if you don't manage them

summary.title: Deck Building and Thinning
bullets: Target deck size: 6-8 focused cards
Destroy via Trade (3 Merchant icons), discard via Workshop 1
Only recruit Townsfolk matching your icon strategy
Tight deck = fast cycling = consistent strong turns

summary.title: Exploiting Building Links
bullets: Links benefit both players — build next to opponents selectively
Strong link bonuses: 2 Silver, resources, Deed/Debt flips

Plan building route around the board for maximum own-link bonuses

summary.title: Poverty vs Prosperity Race
bullets: Poverty revealed: most flipped Deeds scores 12/8/4 VP
Prosperity revealed: most flipped Debts scores 12/8/4 VP
Push toward the pile that benefits your flipped cards
Divided Kingdom (both revealed) rewards players flipping both types

summary.title: Timing the Endgame

Ahead? Accelerate by gaining Debts/Deeds to deplete the pile
Behind? Slow down by avoiding actions that pull from the dwindling pile
Count remaining cards in both piles to plan

summary.title: Maximizing Collision Rewards
bullets: Outer collision positions = weaker rewards; inner = stronger (more Deeds/Debts)
Control collision timing via Corruption/Virtue gain rates
Recruit Townsfolk with collision effects for bonus value

summary.title: Reading the Board State
bullets: Track opponents' icon accumulation, building plans, Viscount positions
Dismiss strong cards to deny opponents' strategies
Monitor Poverty/Prosperity pile depth to decide competitive focus
Board awareness separates reactive from proactive play

entries.definition: Your main piece that moves clockwise around the Main Board. Its position determines which primary action you can take, which Townsfolk you can hire or dismiss, and which Manuscript or Building Spot you can access.

Your personal board with spaces for Townsfolk Cards (left to right), building slots (top), Virtue/Corruption track, and building ability reveals. Cards shift right each turn and drop off the right edge.
The circular board formed by 5 segments with a Castle in the centre. Has an outside path and an inside path, with Townsfolk piles, Manuscript piles, and Building Spots arranged around it.

The 3D structure in the centre of the Main Board with three tiers. Workers are placed in the first tier and cascade inward to the second and third tiers. Workers score VP equal to their tier at game end (1/2/3).

Coloured pieces placed into the Castle. Each player has 20. They score VP equal to the tier they occupy at game end (first tier = 1 VP, second tier = 2 VP, third tier = 3 VP). Bumped Workers return to owner's supply.
The primary currency. Used to hire or dismiss Townsfolk, pay for extra movement, and fund Trade actions. Gained through Trade, collision rewards, building links, bumping, and card effects.
One of 3 resource types (24 in supply). Primarily spent as extra Cleric icons (1 Ink Well = 1 Cleric icon) when transcribing Manuscripts. Gained through Trade, Building Spots, and various effects.
One of 3 resource types (24 in supply). Primarily spent as extra Noble icons (1 Gold = 1 Noble icon) when placing Workers. Gained through Trade, Building Spots, and various effects.
One of 3 resource types (24 in supply). Primarily spent as extra Builder icons (1 Stone = 1 Builder icon) when constructing Buildings. Gained through Trade, Building Spots, and various effects.

Gained from the Poverty pile during collisions and effects. Has two states: unpaid (front) costs -2 VP at game end, and paid (flipped) costs nothing and grants any 1 resource when flipped. Debts deplete the Poverty pile toward endgame.

Gained from the Prosperity pile during collisions and effects. Has two states: acquired (front) scores 1 VP at game end, and approved (flipped) scores 3 VP. Deeds deplete the Prosperity pile toward endgame.

Hidden beneath the top portion of the Debt pile. When revealed (Debts above it are depleted), triggers the endgame. If active at scoring, players with the most flipped Deeds score 12/8/4 VP for 1st/2nd/3rd place.

Hidden beneath the top portion of the Deed pile. When revealed (Deeds above it are depleted), triggers the endgame. If active at scoring, players with the most flipped Debts score 12/8/4 VP for 1st/2nd/3rd place.
When both the Poverty Card and Prosperity Card are revealed in the same game. Both scoring bonuses apply at end-game, rewarding players who have been flipping both Debts and Deeds.

One of 10 cards drafted during setup that determines your starting Viscount position, starting resources, and any special setup effects. Flipped over to serve as a reference card for bumping rewards, shuffle rules, and hand limit.
Awarded to the first player to move a Worker into the third tier of the Castle. Worth 5 VP at game end and grants +1 hand limit. Transfers to any player who later has more Workers in the third tier.
Worth 3 VP at game end. Awarded to the first player to transcribe 3 Manuscripts of the same ribbon colour. There are 4 Cleric Bonus Cards, one per ribbon colour.
Used when any supply (Debts, Deeds, Silver, Ink Wells, Stone, Gold) runs out. Represents larger quantities (e.g., 10 Silver and 5 Gold) to accommodate unlimited supply rules.

Cards that form your deck and sit on your Player Board providing icons. Have a Silver value (top-left), icons (left side), name, immediate/ongoing/drop-off abilities, and a hire/dismiss cost. Three types: Starting (player colour), Hero (brown title), and Neutral (black title).
The 8 player-coloured Townsfolk Cards each player begins with. Generally weaker than Neutral and Hero Townsfolk. Good candidates for destroying to thin your deck.
10 brown-titled Townsfolk Cards, one dealt to each player during setup drafting. Generally stronger than Starting Townsfolk with powerful abilities. Your Hero card is a deck cornerstone.
68 black-titled Townsfolk Cards placed in 5 piles around the Main Board. Can be hired (Phase 4) or dismissed (Phase 3) during the game. Only the top card of each pile is available.
Icon used to fund the Trade action. Each Merchant icon provides 1 unit of trading power. Can be supplemented by spending Silver (1 Silver = 1 extra Merchant icon) or by dismissing Townsfolk.
Icon used to fund the Construct a Building action. Workshop requires 3, Trading Post requires 5, Guildhall requires 7. Can be supplemented by spending Stone (1 Stone = 1 extra Builder icon).
Icon used to fund the Place Workers action. 1/3/5/8 Noble icons place 1/2/3/4 Workers. Can be supplemented by spending Gold (1 Gold = 1 extra Noble icon).
Icon used to fund the Transcribe a Manuscript action. Cost varies by Manuscript (typically 3-6). Can be supplemented by spending Ink Wells (1 Ink Well = 1 extra Cleric icon).

Wild icon that counts as any other single icon type for primary actions only. Does NOT count as other icons for scoring Manuscripts or other non-action purposes. Causes Corruption: 1 Corruption per Criminal on board when playing a Criminal, and 1 Corruption when shuffling with Criminals on board.

An effect on a Townsfolk Card that triggers once when the card is first played to your Player Board or when hired/dismissed. Shown in the top-right area of the card. Resolved immediately upon triggering.
An effect on a Townsfolk Card that is active while the card sits on your Player Board. Positioned above the phase where it is relevant. Does NOT apply during end-game scoring.
An effect on a Townsfolk Card that triggers when the card drops off the right side of your Player Board into your discard pile. Resolved immediately when the card is shifted off.
An effect on a Townsfolk Card that triggers during Phase 5 when a player resolves a collision between their Virtue and Corruption Markers. Shown with a collision icon on the card.

Pay the Silver cost of the faceup Townsfolk adjacent to your Viscount to remove it from the game. You gain its icons for your current primary action and its immediate effect. Only 1 dismiss per turn. The card is removed from the game entirely.
In Phase 4, pay the Silver cost of the faceup Townsfolk adjacent to your Viscount to add it to your discard pile. Resolve its immediate effect. Only 1 hire per turn.
Remove a Townsfolk Card from the game entirely, either from your hand or the top of your Draw Pile. Gain Silver from the supply equal to the destroyed card's Silver value. Always optional.
Rearrange the Townsfolk Cards on your Player Board in any order you wish. This is always optional. Triggered by certain card effects and when an opposing Viscount lands on your space.

Shuffle your discard pile to form a new Draw Pile. Triggered when you need to draw but have no Draw Pile. Must have at least 1 card in discard. Check for Criminals on your Player Board: 1+ Criminals = gain 1 Corruption, 0 Criminals = gain 1 Virtue.

Moves your Virtue Marker one space to the left on your Player Board. Gained from building effects, Townsfolk abilities, bumping from the second tier, shuffling without Criminals, and other sources. When markers collide, triggers a collision in Phase 5.

Moves your Corruption Marker one space to the right on your Player Board. Gained from playing Criminals, shuffling with Criminals on board, and certain card effects. Always resolved before Virtue if both gained simultaneously.

Occurs in Phase 5 when Virtue and Corruption Markers occupy the same space. Resolution order: Workshop 2 check, card effects, current player gains top items, opponents gain bottom items. Both markers reset to opposite ends afterward.

One of 3 building types. Costs 3 Builder icons. Scores 3 VP for 1 built, 5 VP for 2 built. Ongoing abilities: (1st) Discard when hiring, (2nd) +1 Virtue during collision if no Criminals, (3rd) +1 hand limit.

One of 3 building types. Costs 5 Builder icons. Scores 4 VP for 1 built, 9 VP for 2 built, 15 VP for 3 built. Ongoing abilities: (1st) +1 free movement, (2nd) Dismiss cost = 1 Silver, (3rd) Permanent icon.
One of 3 building types. Costs 7 Builder icons. Scores VP similarly to Trading Posts. Ongoing abilities: each Guildhall provides a permanent icon on your Player Board, strengthening your actions.

Designated spaces on the Main Board where buildings can be placed. Divided by a river on each segment: left spaces access left-side spots, right spaces access right-side spots. Each spot has an immediate effect gained when a building is placed there.

A printed connection between two adjacent Building Spots on the Main Board. When a building is placed that completes a link to another player's building, both players gain the link bonus. If you own both buildings on a link, you gain the bonus only once.

A dividing line on each Main Board segment that separates Building Spots into left and right groups. Your Viscount's position on the outside path (left or right space) determines which side of the river you can build on.
The outer ring of spaces on the Main Board. Viscounts on the outside path can Trade or Construct Buildings. Each segment has two outside spaces (left and right).
The inner ring of spaces on the Main Board, closer to the Castle. Viscounts on the inside path can Place Workers or Transcribe Manuscripts. Each segment has two inside spaces.

One of the 5 modular pieces that form the Main Board. Each segment has outside and inside path spaces, Building Spots, a Townsfolk pile, and a Manuscript pile. Segments are placed randomly during setup.

Tiles placed in 5 piles on the Main Board. Transcribed by spending Cleric icons. Score VP from printed values and from sets of different ribbon colours (1/2/3 colours = 1/4/9 VP per set). Grey-backed manuscripts are starting tiles; black-backed are standard.

Each Manuscript has one of several ribbon colours. Sets of different colours score increasing VP (1 colour = 1 VP, 2 colours = 4 VP, 3 colours = 9 VP). First to collect 3 of the same colour claims a Cleric Bonus Card.
Your personal facedown deck of Townsfolk Cards, placed to the left of your Player Board. Draw from it to refill your hand in Phase 6. When empty, shuffle your discard pile to form a new Draw Pile.

Your personal faceup pile of Townsfolk Cards to the right of your Player Board. Cards enter here when they drop off your board or are hired. Shuffled into a new Draw Pile when your Draw Pile is empty.
The maximum number of cards you can hold at the end of Phase 6. Starts at 3. Can be increased by Workshop 3 (+1) and the Castle Leader Card (+1). If already at or above the limit, do not draw.

The outermost ring of the Castle. Workers are placed here initially. When 3+ of your Workers are in a first-tier section, 1 moves inward to the second tier and 1 moves clockwise and 1 counter-clockwise. Bumped Workers from here gain 2 Silver.

The middle ring of the Castle. Workers arrive here from first-tier cascades. When 3+ of your Workers are in a second-tier section, 1 moves inward to the third tier gaining any 1 resource. Workers do NOT move laterally in the second tier. Bumped Workers from here gain 1 Virtue + 1 resource.

The innermost ring of the Castle. Workers arrive here from second-tier cascades, gaining any 1 resource. Workers can never be bumped from the third tier. Each scores 3 VP at game end. Most Workers in the third tier = Castle Leader Card.

After all Castle movements, any section with more than 3 Workers (regardless of colour) must be reduced to 3. The current player chooses which Workers are bumped. Bumped Workers return to their owner's supply with compensation (first tier: 2 Silver; second tier: 1 Virtue + 1 resource).
An effect that lets you hire any 1 visible Townsfolk Card from the Main Board without paying its Silver cost. The card goes to your discard pile and its immediate effect is resolved.
An effect that lets you gain 1 Manuscript costing 3 or 4 Cleric icons from those visible on the Main Board, without paying any Cleric icons or Ink Wells.
An effect that lets you place a building for free on any available Building Spot on the Main Board, without paying Builder icons or Stone.
An effect that lets you move 1 of your Workers from one first-tier Castle section to an adjacent first-tier section. Always optional.
Turning a Debt from unpaid to paid (gaining any 1 resource) or a Deed from acquired to approved (upgrading from 1 VP to 3 VP). Can target Debt, Deed, or player's choice depending on the effect.
Given to the randomly chosen first player during setup. Remains with that player for the entire game (does not rotate). The player to the right of the start player always has the last turn.
